interviewProcess: Round 1: Phone interview (Talent Scout): Questions from projects, merge sort vs quick sort, sorting Person objects by age.

Round 2: Phone interview + coding on shared Google Doc (Software Developer): Questions from projects (bi-directional sync), checking if a number can be represented in 5^n form, removing unnecessary spaces from a string.
Round 3: Phone interview + coding on shared Google Doc (Software Developer): Optimizing a^b, splitting a search query across multiple machines, developing the game Snake (data structures and coding).
Telephonic Interviews (Two Rounds): 45-minute interviews on the phone, interviewer shares a Google Doc, asks one medium or two easy Algo DS problems.
Onsite Interviews: 5 interviews, 4 Algo DS interviews and 1 Googliness (behavioral) interview.
Team Matching Round: Recruiter contacts candidate with feedback, profile goes to different teams for matching based on work style and interests.
dsaTopics: Minimum Domino Rotations For Equal Row
Longest Substring with At Most Two Distinct Characters
Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters
Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column
Minimum Cost to Hire K Workers
Minimize Max Distance to Gas Station
behavioralQuestions: Why should one use merge sort over quick sort and vice-versa.
Questions from Project: How it works , Bigram and ngram model approach, etc.
Questions from Project: Bi-directional Sync b/w mysql and sqilte db.
What data structures will you use to develop the game Snake?
How would you split a search query across multiple machines?
enrichedInsights: Google's interview process is rigorous, involving multiple rounds of technical and behavioral assessments. The company emphasizes strong DSA skills, with a significant focus on problem-solving efficiency and clear communication of thought processes. Candidates are advised to think aloud, explain their rationale, clarify questions, and iterate on solutions, even starting with a brute-force approach. Red flags might include not clarifying questions or failing to explain thought processes. The overall experience aims to assess not just technical ability but also "Googliness," suggesting a culture that values collaborative and thoughtful engineers.
